data.title: Japan November factory output falls 2.6% month-on-month
data.description:
Japan's factory output fell 2.6% in November from the previous month, slightly worse than the median market forecast for a 2.0% drop, government data showed on Friday.

Manufacturers surveyed by the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry expect seasonally adjusted output to grow 1.3% in December and jump 8.0% in January.

data.title: Tokyo Consumer Inflation Slows But Remains Above BOJ's Target
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--Consumer inflation in the Tokyo metropolitan area slowed in December but remained above the Bank of Japan's 2% target, keeping the central bank on track for further monetary tightening.

Consumer prices in Tokyo, excluding fresh food, rose 2.3% year-over-year, easing from November's 2.8% increase, government data showed on Friday.

The reading was lower than the 2.5% increase expected in a poll of economists by data provider Quick.

Tokyo figures are widely viewed as a leading indicator of nationwide inflation trends.

The Japanese central bank last week raised its policy rate by 25 basis points to 0.75%, the highest level in 30 years, reflecting confidence that the nation's wage-price mechanism is functioning smoothly.

BOJ Gov. Kazuo Ueda has said that real interest rates remain significantly low even after the latest increase, pledging to adjust monetary policy further if economic and price data align with the central bank's projections.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Japan Industrial Output Falls More than Estimated
data.description:
Japan’s industrial production dropped 2.6% month-on-month in November 2025, reversing a 1.5% increase in the previous month and coming in weaker than market expectations of a 2.0% decline, according to flash data.

The contraction marked the steepest drop since January 2024.

data.title: Gold Edges Higher Amid Geopolitical Risks — Market Talk
data.description: Gold edges higher in the morning Asian session amid geopolitical risks. Six months after launching a war with Iran, Israeli officials are raising prospects of another clash over Tehran's efforts to rebuild its arsenal of ballistic missiles. Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu said this week that Israel was watching Hamas, Hezbollah, and Iran as they rearm and would act if necessary. Gold remains supported by sustained demand for safe-haven assets, Tickmill's Joseph Dahrieh says in an email. Geopolitical risks are elevated across the Middle East, Eastern Europe, Asia, and Latin America, the managing principal adds. Spot gold is 0.3% higher at $4,493.63/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Nikkei May Trade in Range; Yen in Focus — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks may be rangebound as trading thins near the year end. Nikkei futures are flat at 50490 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 155.79, compared with 155.81 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any progress in Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's economic plans as well as any comments on the yen's recent depreciation from Japanese government officials. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.1% to 50407.79 on Thursday.(kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

data.title: Japan Jobless Rate Stays at 2.6% for 4th Month
data.description:
Japan’s unemployment rate stood at 2.6% in November 2025, unchanged from the previous three months and in line with market estimates.

It remained the highest reading since July 2024.

data.title: 55% of the companies who raised funds via QIPs in 2025 trade below their issue price
data.description:
As many as 55% of the companies who raised funds through the Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP) route in 2025 are trading below the price at which they issued shares to eligible institutions, according to the latest study by Motilal Oswal.

After a record year in 2024, where companies raised ₹1.36 lakh crore through the QIP route, the figure has seen a dip this year, with ₹71,800 crore being mobilized by 35 companies, according to the study.

India's largest lender, State Bank of India (SBI) has contributed to 35% of this tally with its record ₹25,000 crore QIP, while companies such as Swiggy, Biocon, CG Power and Hitachi Energy featuring among the top 10 companies, that comprised of 76% of the total funds raised this year.

However, only one company in this list, Centum Electronics, has doubled from its QIP issue price, with returns of 101% so far. SBI, after its record fund raise, has seen its stock jump nearly 20%, in-line with the surge seen in PSU banks.

Among the underperformers include IREDA, who raised ₹2,000 crore after issuing shares at ₹165 apiece. The stock is down 18% from those levels, along with names like Kaynes Tech, whose shares are down 25% from their QIP issue price of ₹5,570.

Small state-run lenders, with low free float in the market, who also mobilized funds through the QIP route this year, such as Indian Overseas Bank, Punjab & Sind Bank, Central Bank of India, and UCO Bank, have also seen their shares drop 17%, 29%, 7% and 15% from their respective issue price.

Shares of other smaller companies, such as Rajoo Engineers, Genesys International, Shakti Pumps, who raised between ₹200 to ₹300 crore through the QIP route, have seen their shares drop 41%, 31% and 22% respectively from their issue price, according to the Motilal Oswal study.

Among the outperformers, apart from Centum Electronics, only Hitachi Energy has delivered returns in excess of 50% from their QIP issue price, with a 63% gain so far. Other stocks with positive returns include Azad Engineering (Up 31%), Navin Fluorine (Up 26%) and Biocon (Up 21%.)

data.title: Yomiuri: Japan Eager to Obtain Carbon Credits from Deals with Emerging Nations
data.description:
By Takashi Itoda

Yomiuri Shimbun Correspondent

BANGKOK — The Japanese government is working on obtaining cooperation from Southeast Asian countries and other emerging nations on a carbon credit system, which allows trading of reductions in carbon dioxide emissions.

The government aims to achieve the country's decarbonization goal with the help of the system, which enables CO2 reduction efforts conducted by Japanese companies abroad to be counted for reduction in Japan.

However, concerns have been raised that competition may intensify among advanced nations to secure carbon credits from deals with emerging nations.

Japan has set a goal of reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 46% compared to the fiscal 2013 levels by fiscal 2030, which ends March 2031. However, energy-saving efforts have progressed significantly in Japan, leaving little room for further CO2 reduction. In contrast, decarbonization efforts are still under progress in emerging nations, offering an opportunity for large-scale reductions.

In 2013, the government introduced a framework for carbon credit trading called the Joint Crediting Mechanism (JCM). Japan has concluded agreements with 31 countries, including India and Mexico. Under the system, reductions in CO2, achieved in partner countries through efforts such as introducing renewable energy, will be transferred to Japan as credits. To incentivize companies, the government subsidizes up to 50% of the initial investment cost when Japanese companies invest in decarbonization-related projects in partner countries. About 300 decarbonization projects are now progressing.

Article 6 of the Paris Agreement, the international framework for combating global warming, stipulates a mechanism that allows countries to count carbon credits gained abroad toward their own reduction targets. This mechanism took full effect in November 2024. It requires certain procedures for JCM's credits to be eligible under Article 6 of the Paris Agreement, such as verification of the effectiveness of CO2 reduction by third-party organizations.

In November 2025, the Japanese government agreed with the Thai government on a transfer of credits. The credits will be the first obtained under Article 6. Credits will be gained from the generation of electricity by solar panels installed by Tokyo-based photovoltaic company TSB GreeNex Co. on a wastewater treatment reservoir at an industrial park in Thailand.

Hiroshi Ono, a senior advisor to Japan's Environment Ministry, expressed his intention to make other JCM projects eligible for Article 6 at a ceremony to celebrate the agreement held on Dec. 16 in Bangkok. "The JCM has generated investments worth 3 billion dollars worldwide," he said.

Transfer limited to half

However, the amount of credits to be transferred to Japan from Thailand will be 1,009 tons, which accounts for only half of the total annual reduction of 2,017 tons. "Thailand refused to transfer the full amount, as it had concerns that it might hinder its own goal in achieving decarbonization," a Japanese government source said.

Prior to the agreement with Japan, Thailand struck a deal with Switzerland to transfer credits to the European nation in exchange for Swiss support in introducing electric buses in Bangkok. It implies Thailand's intention to utilize decarbonization as a springboard to obtain economic benefits by encouraging other nations to compete in making investments.

Credits spreading in advanced nations

Moves to secure carbon credits are spreading among advanced nations. Switzerland plans to cover one-third of its CO2 reduction targets with carbon credits obtained overseas, signing memorandums and agreements with 17 countries including Thailand. Singapore leads a framework aimed at vitalizing carbon credit trading named "Coalition to Grow Carbon Markets," which comprises 10 nations including Peru and Kenya.

In Japan, a CO2 emissions trading system between domestic companies will be implemented fully in fiscal 2026. To activate emissions trading between domestic companies, it will be important for the companies to increase credits obtained from abroad.

"Initiatives similar to the JCM are spreading in other countries," said Nami Kuwana, a senior fellow at the Marubeni Institute. "It is necessary to establish an environment in which companies and partner countries will be willing to take part in the JCM, such as by simplifying procedures."

----

This article is from The Yomiuri Shimbun. Neither Dow Jones Newswires, MarketWatch, Barron's nor The Wall Street Journal were involved in the creation of this content.

YDN-M0000168030-1

data.title: Tokyo Core Inflation Below Forecasts
data.description:
Core consumer prices in Tokyo’s Ku-area rose 2.3% year-on-year in December 2025, slowing from a 2.8% in the previous month and falling short of market estimates of 2.5%.

It was the lowest reading since February.

However, the latest result remained above the Bank of Japan’s 2% target, underscoring persistent price pressures in the capital.

data.title: Yomiuri: Fujitsu Expects Expansion in Defense Business, Positive on Collaboration
data.description:
By Chika Nakayama

Yomiuri Shimbun Staff Writer

Fujitsu Ltd. expects to see expansion in its defense-related business as the Japanese government is increasing defense spending in such areas as Self-Defense Forces equipment, CEO Takahito Tokita said during an interview with The Yomiuri Shimbun.

"The role of AI, quantum and other advanced technologies in national security will certainly grow. I believe Fujitsu's responsibility will also increase," Tokita said.

Fujitsu handles defense equipment in communications devices, as well as information and communications systems and other areas.

"We will continue our efforts to ensure our added value is recognized," Tokita said.

Citing the partnership announced in May with Lockheed Martin Corp., under which Fujitsu supplies radar components, Tokita expressed an intention for the company to collaborate domestically or internationally if it finds economic rationale in doing so.

Regarding Rapidus Corp., a Japanese company aiming to domestically produce advanced semiconductors, Tokita said, "We are aware (Fujitsu) is one of the few domestic users of their products and will support them."

"It is extremely important for there to be a production base (for advanced semiconductors) within the nation. We prefer to use domestically produced (chips), so we expect their performance to continue improving," Tokita said.

As for investing in Rapidus, he said, "We will help, but nothing has been decided."

The interview was conducted on Monday.

----

This article is from The Yomiuri Shimbun. Neither Dow Jones Newswires, MarketWatch, Barron's nor The Wall Street Journal were involved in the creation of this content.

YDN-M0000168035-1

data.title: Core inflation in Japan's capital slows in December but stays above BOJ target
data.description:
Key points:
  • Tokyo December core CPI up 2.3% yr/yr vs forecast +2.5%
  • Index excluding fresh food, fuel rises 2.6% yr/yr in December
  • BOJ next meets for policy meeting January 22-23

By Leika Kihara

Core consumer prices in Japan's capital rose 2.3% in December from a year earlier, data showed on Friday, staying above the central bank's 2% target and firming the case for further interest rate hikes.

The increase in the Tokyo core consumer price index, which excludes volatile costs of fresh food, compared with a median market forecast for a 2.5% rise. It slowed from a 2.8% increase in November, due largely to a fall in utility bills.

A separate index for Tokyo that strips away both fresh food and fuel costs - closely watched by the Bank of Japan as a measure of demand-driven prices - rose 2.6% in December from a year earlier after a 2.8% increase in November.

The data will be among the factors the BOJ will scrutinise at its next policy meeting on January 22 and 23, when the board issues fresh quarterly growth and inflation forecasts.

The BOJ raised interest rates last week to a 30-year high of 0.75%, taking another landmark step in ending decades of huge monetary support in a sign of its conviction Japan is progressing toward durably hitting its 2% inflation target.

With core inflation exceeding the BOJ's target for nearly four years, Governor Kazuo Ueda has signalled the BOJ's readiness to keep raising rates if the economy continues to improve, backed by solid wage gains.

Some analysts say the yen's recent declines could add to inflationary pressure through rising import costs, a point a few BOJ board members flagged at last week's policy meeting.
